One thing I’ve learned after working on Angular applications for years is this:
Your application can feel incredibly fast during development and still become painfully slow in production.
That’s one of the reasons Angular performance issues are so frustrating. During development, you test with maybe 20 records. Production has 200,000. You test with one browser tab. Users open twelve. Your API responds in 100ms. Production occasionally responds in 4 seconds. Everything looked fine.
Until it wasn’t.
The worst Angular performance problems aren’t the ones that show up immediately. They’re the ones hiding behind “working” code that quietly becomes expensive as your application grows. This article isn’t about obvious mistakes like forgetting trackBy or calling functions in templates (although we'll discuss those too). It’s about the performance traps that wait until your application has real users, real data, and real complexity.
Development Data Lies to You
This is probably the biggest reason performance problems survive until production.
Imagine a customer list.
customers = [
{
id: 1,
name: 'Alice'
},

name: 'Bob'…
